publishedAbstract: Leveraging secondary dedicated networks with ODF through the use of Multus CNI can bring advantages like enhanced bandwidth and security. Out of the box ODF uses vanilla OVNKubernetes network which can be accepted in STCs (Single Tenant Clusters) as the whole cluster is dedicated to either one vendor or one application. The use of OVNKubernetes network can become challenging in MTCs (Multi-tenant Clusters) where different workloads are running for different vendors with emphasis on isolation using approaches like network policies along side other physical and/or software segregation techniques. However, if the default OVNKubernetes network is used this could lead to some apps-especially ones with elevated privileges and relaxed SCCs- being able to sniff data on the fly whether data being written to a PVC or replication traffic across storage nodes. Openshift clusters run multiple nodes, usually having dedicated nodes for control plane, storage and applications (worker nodes). From a security perspective using two separate physical networks for storage would make it physically impossible for apps running on worker nodes to sniff replication traffic as these nodes will not be equipped with an interface to connect to the replication network. For enhanced security, bear in mind the storage networks will not be routable. Cluster administrators have the option of deploying public-network-only, cluster-network-only, or public-and-cluster-network-both. As a general starting point, it's likely that most customers would be best served starting from the position that they will use a public-network-only install. This could be a good starting recommendation because it achieves isolation and performance boost without adding too much extra cost/complexity. Adding the cluster network as well means that replication traffic will have dedicated bandwidth separate from client I/O, but the downside is that there is a lot more wasted bandwidth on average when all 3 networks are used. And users may need to buy extra NICs to accommodate networking requirements. OSD replication tunable parameters could be set such that failure events wouldn't bring client I/O bandwidth down lower than some SLO. However, fine-tuning these paremaete could be difficult to control in an opinionated ODF deployment. When things are healthy, client I/O should be able to use all the bandwidth that would otherwise be reserved solely for use by the cluster net. That said, it is important to make sure an ODF cluster is eligible to use dedicated secondary networks by verifying the network performance and configuration before migrating an existing ODF cluster to use dedicated secondary networks or before installing ODF. A CLI tool known as the rook CLI tool was developed with that objective in mind. The tool has evolved over time, currently it is available within the rook-ceph-operator, it can be downloaded from the operator pod under /usr/local/bin/rook. However, it is strongly advised that users will download the tool attached to [article #7014721](https://access.redhat.com/articles/7014721) due to known issues with docker hub image rate limiting.
publishedTitle: rook multus validation tool use cases
